                   2
                       Spendable earnings are calculated by taking the average weekly pay for all production
                or nonsupervisory jobs, both full-time and part-time, and then deducting social security and

                                                                                               Sampling error. The standard error is primarily a measure of sampl-
                  There are two types of errors possible in an estimate based on a          ing variability, that is, of the variation that occurs by chance because a
               sample survey—sampling and nonsampling. The standard errors pro-             sample rather than the entire population is surveyed. The sample
               vided primarily indicate the magnitude of the sampling error. They           estimate and its estimated standard error enable one to construct con-
               also partially measure the effect of some nonsampling errors in              fidence intervals, ranges that would include the average of all possible
               response and enumeration but do not measure any systematic biases in         samples with a known probability. For example, if all possible
               the data.                                                                    samples were selected, each of these surveyed under essentially the
                  Nonsampling errors. The full extent of nonsampling error is               same general conditions and using the sample sample design, and an
               unknown, but special studies have been conducted to quantify some            estimate and its estimated error were calculated from each sample,
               sources of nonsampling error in the CPS as discussed below. The ef-          then:

               change, such as month-to-month change. Estimates of monthly levels           ror below the estimate to one standard error above the estimate would
               would be more severely affected by the nonsampling error.                    include the average result of all possible samples.
                  Nonsampling errors in surveys can be attributed to many sources,             2. Approximately 90 percent of the intervals from 1.6 standard er-
               e.g., inability to obtain information about all cases in the sample,         rors below the estmate to 1.6 standard errors above the estimate would
               definitional difficulties, differences in the interpretation of questions,   include the average of all possile samples.
               inability or unwillingness of respondents to provide correct informa-           3. Approximately 95 percent of the intervals from 2 standard errors

                One measure of the reliability of the employment estimates for in-        Table Q. Errors of preliminary employment estimates
              dividual industries is the root-mean-square error (RMSE). The
              measure is the standard deviation adjusted for the bias in estimates:                                                      Root- mean- square
                                                                                                                                            error of—
                   RMSE =      \/(Standard Deviation)2 + (Bias)2
                                                                                                          Category                                   Month-to-
                                                                                                                                       Monthly
              If the bias is small, the chances are about 2 out of 3 that an estimate                                                                 month
                                                                                                                                        level
              from the sample would differ from its benchmark by less than the                                                                        change
              root-mean-square error. The chances are about 19 out of 20 that the
              difference would be less than twice the root-mean-square error.

                                                              Seasonal adjustment

                  Over a course of a year, the size of the Nation's labor force, the          All civilian labor force and unemployment rate statistics, as well as
               levels of employment and unemployment, and other measures of                the major employment and unemployment estimates, are computed
               labor market activity undergo sharp fluctuations due to such seasonal       by aggregating independently adjusted series. For example, for each
               events as changes in weather, reduced or expanded production,               of the three major labor force components—agricultural employment,
               harvests, major holidays, and the opening and closing of schools.           nonagricultural employment, and unemployment—data for four sex-
               Because these seasonal events follow a more or less regular pattern         age groups (males and females under and over 20 years of age) are
               each year, their influence on statistical trends can be eliminated by ad-   separately adjusted for seasonal variation and are then added to derive
               justing the statistics from month to month. These adjustments make it       seasonally adjusted total figures. In order to provide seasonally ad-
               easier to observe the cyclical and other nonseasonal movements in the       justed total employment and civilian labor force estimates, the ap-
               series. In evaluating changes in a seasonally adjusted series, it is im-    propriate series are aggregated. The official unemployment rate for all
               portant to note that seasonal adjustment is merely an approximation         civilian workers is derived by dividing the estimate for total unemploy-
               based on past experience. Seasonally adjusted estimates have a              ment (the sum of 4 seasonally adjusted sex-age components) by the
               broader margin of possible error than the original data on which they       civilian labor force (the sum of 12 seasonally adjusted sex-age com-
               are based, since they are subject not only to sampling and other errors     ponents).
               but are also affected by the uncertainties of the seasonal adjustment          Revised seasonally adjusted data for selected labor force series bas-
               process itself. Seasonally adjusted series for selected labor force and     ed on the experience through December 1979, new seasonal adjust-
               establishment data are published regularly in Employment and Earn-          ment factors to be used to calculate the overall unemployment rate for
               ings.                                                                       the first 6 months of 1980, and a description of the current seasonal
